message.abstract:
<jats:title>Summary</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Angiotensin converting enzyme 2 (
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    ) serves as the host receptor for SARS-CoV-2, with a critical role in viral infection. We aim to understand population level variation of nasopharyngeal
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    expression in people tested for COVID-19 and the relationship between
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    expression and SARS-CoV-2 viral RNA load, while adjusting for expression of the complementary protease, Transmembrane serine protease 2 (
                    <jats:italic>TMPRSS2)</jats:italic>
                    , soluble
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    , age, and biological sex.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    A cross-sectional study of n=424 participants aged 1-104 years referred for COVID-19 testing was performed in British Columbia, Canada. Participants who tested negative or positive for COVID-19 were matched by age and biological sex. Viral and host gene expression was measured by quantitative reverse-transcriptase polymerase chain reaction. Bivariate analysis and multiple linear regression were performed to understand the role of nasopharyngeal
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    expression in SARS-CoV-2 infection. The
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    gene was targeted to measure expression of transmembrane and soluble transcripts.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Findings</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Analysis shows no association between age and nasopharyngeal
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    expression in those who tested negative for COVID-19 (P=0·092). Mean expression of transmembrane (P=1·2e-4), soluble
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    (P<0·0001) and
                    <jats:italic>TMPRSS2</jats:italic>
                    (P<0·0001) differed between COVID-19-negative and -positive groups. In bivariate analysis of COVID-19-positive participants, expression of transmembrane
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    positively correlated with SARS-CoV-2 RNA viral load (P<0·0001), expression of soluble
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    negatively correlated (P<0·0001), and no correlation was found with
                    <jats:italic>TMPRSS2 (P=</jats:italic>
                    0·694
                    <jats:italic>)</jats:italic>
                    . Multivariable analysis showed that the greatest viral RNA loads were observed in participants with high transmembrane
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    expression (
                    <jats:italic>B</jats:italic>
                    =0·886, 95%CI:[0·596 to 1·18]), while expression of soluble
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    may protect against high viral RNA load in the upper respiratory tract (
                    <jats:italic>B</jats:italic>
                    = −0·0990, 95%CI:[−0·176 to −0·0224]).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Interpretation</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Nasopharyngeal
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    expression plays a dual, contrasting role in SARS-CoV-2 infection of the upper respiratory tract. Transmembrane
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    positively correlates, while soluble
                    <jats:italic>ACE2</jats:italic>
                    negatively correlates with viral RNA load after adjusting for age, biological sex and expression of TMPRSS2.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Funding</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This project (COV-55) was funded by Genome British Columbia as part of their COVID-19 rapid response initiative.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
